title: Area under density curves
description: Calculate the area under regions of simple density curves.

assessment_items.question_content:
Consider the density curve below. 

![A density curve is plotted on an x y coordinate plane. Two line segments form the curve. The first increases from (1, 0) to (2, 0.5). The second decreases from (2, 0.5) to (5, 0). All values estimated.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/a19a78d765faecdbba59f6d516c5e375ce55ee06)

**Find the percent of the area under the density curve where $x$ is less than $2$.**

[[☃ numeric-input 1]]$\%$
hints:
By definition, the area under a complete density curve is equal to $1$. So, the percent of the total area under a portion of the curve is equal to the area under that part of the curve.

We are looking for the area under the density curve when $x<2$:

![A density curve is plotted on an x y coordinate plane. Two line segments form the curve. The first increases from (1, 0) to (2, 0.5). The second decreases from (2, 0.5) to (5, 0). The area beneath the curve to the left of x = 2 is shaded. All values estimated.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/899e7a38f0c182087db3bf10ef4e982bc7e8aba2)

We can use the area formula for a triangle to find this area:

$\text{Area} = \dfrac12 (\text{base}) (\text{height})$

**Find the percent of the area under the density curve where $x$ is greater than $2$.**

[[☃ numeric-input 1]]$\%$
hints:
By definition, the area under a complete density curve is equal to $1$. So, the percent of the total area under a portion of the curve is equal to the area under that part of the curve.

We are looking for the area under the density curve when $x>2$:

![A density curve is plotted on an x y coordinate plane. Two line segments form the curve. The first increases from (1, 0) to (2, 0.5). The second decreases from (2, 0.5) to (5, 0). The area beneath the curve to the right of x = 2 is shaded. All values estimated.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/54e61ef8877cfa90354da6ec05df687bca641014)

We can use the area formula for a triangle to find this area:

$\text{Area} = \dfrac12 (\text{base}) (\text{height})$

assessment_items.question_content:
Consider the density curve below. 

![A density curve is plotted on an x y coordinate plane. The curve is a horizontal line segment which extends horizontally from (1, 0.25) to (5, 0.25). Dashed vertical lines rise from the x-axis at x = 1 and x = 5, meeting each end of the segment. All values estimated.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/7a5c2776c0bf757a1b4a3a7e9012a03f30a05466)

**Find the percent of the area under the density curve where $x$ is less than $4$.**

[[☃ numeric-input 1]]$\%$
hints:
By definition, the area under a complete density curve is equal to $1$. So, the percent of the total area under a portion of the curve is equal to the area under that part of the curve.

We are looking for the area under the density curve when $x < 4$:

![A density curve is plotted on an x y coordinate plane. The curve is a horizontal line segment which extends horizontally from (1, 0.25) to (5, 0.25). Dashed vertical lines rise from the x-axis at x = 1 and x = 5, meeting each end of the segment. The area beneath the curve between x = 1 and x = 4 is shaded. All values estimated.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/742112e3011be667fa38e701b3ff995edead31d6)

We can use the area formula for a rectangle to find this area:

$\text{Area} =  (\text{length}) (\text{width})$

assessment_items.question_content:
Consider the density curve below. 

![A density curve is plotted on an x y coordinate plane. Two line segments form the curve. The first increases from the origin to (3, 0.4). The second decreases from (3, 0.4) to (5, 0). All values estimated.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/4e49cc7675d73104ae68af683648b03cfa17984b)

**Find the percent of the area under the density curve where $x$ is less than $3$.**

[[☃ numeric-input 1]]$\%$
hints:
By definition, the area under a complete density curve is equal to $1$. So, the percent of the total area under a portion of the curve is equal to the area under that part of the curve.

We are looking for the area under the density curve when $x < 3$:

![A density curve is plotted on an x y coordinate plane. Two line segments form the curve. The first increases from the origin to (3, 0.4). The second decreases from (3, 0.4) to (5, 0). The area under the curve to the left of x = 3 is shaded. All values estimated.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/c097a57f9975f03e4dc74e3a1818c62914ae6e1d)

We can use the area formula for a triangle to find this area:

$\text{Area} = \dfrac{1}{2} (\text{base}) (\text{height})$

All values estimated.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/425c905a3acc3cb14bf648d88b91206259343b26)

A dashed vertical line segment rises from the x-axis at 6, meeting the curve at (6, 0.4). All values estimated.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/df0d7e213ab67b8f8aef9cffdd7355bcf2750d1a)

[[☃ numeric-input 1]]$\%$
hints:
By definition, the area under a complete density curve is equal to $1$. So, the percent of the total area under a portion of the curve is equal to the area under that part of the curve.

We are looking for the area under the density curve when $x < 3$:

![A density curve is plotted on an x y coordinate plane. Two line segments form the curve. The first increases from (3, 0) to (4, 0.4). The second extends horizontally from (4, 0.4) to (6, 0.4). A dashed vertical line segment rises from the x-axis at 6, meeting the curve at (6, 0.4). All values estimated.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/56023dedfb4a14debc8de2c49a68474e34957a16)

There is no area under the density curve when $x<3$.

hints:

We are looking for the area under the density curve when $x > 3$:

![A density curve is plotted on an x y coordinate plane. Two line segments form the density curve. The first segment increases from (3, 0) to (4, 0.4). The other extends horizontally from (4, 0.4) to (6, 0.4). A dashed vertical line segment rises from the x-axis at 6, meeting the curve at (6, 0.4). The area under the curve to the left of x = 6 is shaded. All values estimated.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/ec3fbafcda1a479363ffdecc4c9c73bc3dde2837)

That is the entire area, which is $100\%$ of the area.
